title: Getters and setters should access the expected fields
description: Getters and setters should access the expected fieldsAnalyze your codeintentionality-clearreliabilityBugpitfallWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?More InfoProperties provide a way to enforceencapsulationby providing accessors that give controlled access toprivatefields. However, in classes with multiple fields, it is not unusual thatcopy-and-pasteis used to quickly create the needed properties, which can result in the wrong field being accessed by a getter or setter.class C { private int x; private int y; public int Y => x; // Noncompliant: The returned field should be 'y' }This rule raises an issue in any of these cases:A getter does not access the field with the corresponding name.A setter does not update the field with the corresponding name.For simple properties, it is better to use
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: set { y = value; } // Noncompliant: setting wrong field

logic.detection_conditions: Detects properties with explicit getter/setter bodies (candidates for field access verification)
logic.triggering_conditions: Identifies properties with explicit get/set blocks as candidates for manual review
logic.reasoning: This rule requires semantic analysis to properly match property names to accessed field names. Challenges: 1) Extract property name, 2) Extract field names from getter/setter bodies, 3) Account for naming conventions (_field, m_field, field for Property Field), 4) Verify correspondence. Regex alone cannot reliably achieve this. This implementation provides a heuristic by detecting properties with explicit get/set blocks that access fields (return/assign pattern), flagging them for manual verification. A complete solution requires a custom function with AST traversal and symbol table lookup.

should_trigger: public int Y { get { return x; } }
public int X { set { y = value; } }
should_not_trigger: public int X { get { return _x; } set { _x = value; } }
